coredata.dc:title: Long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent complex of B, N, P co-doped carbon dots and silica for afterglow imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Lin
Zhao, Shaoban
Wang, Yan
Yu, Shiping
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials based on carbon dots (CDs) have attracted abundant attention in bioimaging owing to their long lifetimes and wide Stokes shift, which can reduce the autofluorescence and light scattering in tissue. Although solid-state RTP CDs have been widely reported at present, long-lived RTP CDs in aqueous solutions still remain a challenge because they are usually quenched by oxygen in water. This work develops a heteroatom doping strategy to synthesize long-lived matrix-free phosphorescent B, N, P co-doped CDs (B, N, P-CDs) with cross-linked structure and rich hydrogen bonding network. B, N, P-CDs have an ultralong phosphorescence lifetime of 1.89 s. Subsequently, the complex of B, N, P co-doped CDs and silica (B, N, P-CDs@SiO2) was further obtained by coating the surfaces of B, N, P-CDs with silica by sol-gel method to further confine their molecular vibrations and rotations, which shows an excellent RTP property in aqueous solution with a phosphorescent lifetime of 1.97 s. In addition, the complex displays good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and ability of phosphorescent labeling cells and organism. All these results extend a potential application of the B, N, P-CDs@SiO2 complex as phosphorescent bioimaging probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Long-lived
Carbon dots
Phosphorescent imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and optical properties of B, N, P-CDs280
Effects of heteroatom covalent structure on the long-lived phosphorescent properties of B, N, P-CDs
Structure and optical properties of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2
Bioimaging properties of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2

Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory for Electronic Functional Materials and Devices ( EFMD2021006M ).

Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory for Electronic Functional Materials and Devices (EFMD2021006M).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs of B, N, P-CDs280 in powder form taken under a UV lamp (365 nm) ON and OFF (from 1 to 18 s); Structure and optical properties characterization of B, N, P-CDs280: (b) TEM and HRTEM images. The inset is the size distributions; (c) XRD patterns; (d) FTIR spectra; (e) XPS spectra; (f) UV absorption spectra (purple line), excitation spectra of fluorescence and phosphorescence (blue line and red line), emission spectra of fluorescence and phosphorescence under excitation at 365 nm (green line and yellow line); (g) Fluorescence emission spectra; (h) Phosphorescence emission spectra; (i) Phosphorescence decay curve and fitting curve under ambient conditions (λex = 345 nm and λem = 502 nm).

Phosphorescent emission mechanism of solid-state phosphorescent CDs: (a) Schematic diagrams of structures of B, N, P-CDs200, B, N, P-CDs240, and B, N, P-CDs280; (b) Energy levels of B, N, P-CDs as the reaction temperature increases.

(a) Photographs of B, N, P-CDs280 and B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 solution taken under a UV lamp (365 nm) ON and OFF (from 1 to 12 s); structure and optical properties characterization of B, N, P-CDs280: (b) TEM image; (c) FTIR spectra; (d) XPS spectra; (e) High-resolution XPS spectra of Si 2p; (f) UV absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra, and phosphorescence excitation spectra; (g) Fluorescence emission spectra; (h) Phosphorescence emission spectra; (i) Phosphorescence decay curve and fitting curve under ambient conditions.
Illustration of the phosphorescence formation of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 in aqueous solution: (a) Structure of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2; (b) Proposed mechanism of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2.

Co-localization imaging of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 in HepG2 cells. (a) Bright field image; (b) Fluorescent image; (c) Phosphorescent image; (d) Merged image. Scale bar: 100 µm; (e) Phosphorescent images of the B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 aqueous solution with different concentration; (f) Phosphorescence imaging of two mice with the subcutaneous injection of water and B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 solution, respectively. The circles indicate the injection location.
Schematic diagram of synthesis and applications of B, N, P-CDs@SiO2.
Comparison of the RTP lifetime between B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 and other CDs-based RTP materials in aqueous solution.

Ethylenediamine,
phosphoric acid,
and boric acid

head.ce:title: Long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent complex of B, N, P co-doped carbon dots and silica for afterglow imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials based on carbon dots (CDs) have attracted abundant attention in bioimaging owing to their long lifetimes and wide Stokes shift, which can reduce the autofluorescence and light scattering in tissue. Although solid-state RTP CDs have been widely reported at present, long-lived RTP CDs in aqueous solutions still remain a challenge because they are usually quenched by oxygen in water. This work develops a heteroatom doping strategy to synthesize long-lived matrix-free phosphorescent B, N, P co-doped CDs (B, N, P-CDs) with cross-linked structure and rich hydrogen bonding network. B, N, P-CDs have an ultralong phosphorescence lifetime of 1.89 s. Subsequently, the complex of B, N, P co-doped CDs and silica (B, N, P-CDs@SiO2) was further obtained by coating the surfaces of B, N, P-CDs with silica by sol-gel method to further confine their molecular vibrations and rotations, which shows an excellent RTP property in aqueous solution with a phosphorescent lifetime of 1.97 s. In addition, the complex displays good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and ability of phosphorescent labeling cells and organism. All these results extend a potential application of the B, N, P-CDs@SiO2 complex as phosphorescent bioimaging probe.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This work developed a new strategy for the synthesis of long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent complex of B, N, P co-doped CDs and silica. The phosphorescent lifetime of B, N, P-CDs@SiO2 in aqueous solution was increased to 1.97 s, which can be used for afterglow bioimaging.Long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent complex of B, N, P-CDs@SiO2 was developed for afterglow imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent imaging has attracted a great deal of attention in the biomedical field owing to its excellent resolution, high sensitivity and ability to image and detect the organism both in vivo and in vitro [1–5]. However, one of the inevitable problems with fluorescent imaging is the interference from biological autofluorescence, which can overlap the fluorescence of fluorescent probe, resulting in less imaging accuracy [6]. In addition, fluorescent imaging also requires real-time excitation, while excitation for long time can cause damage to the organism [7].

The emergence of phosphorescent imaging provides a means to solve these problems [8,9]. Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have a long luminescence lifetime and can continue to emit light for a period of time after the excitation source is stopped [10,11]. Therefore, bioimaging can be achieved when the excitation source is switched off, avoiding the interference of the biological autofluorescence and the damages caused by exciting light. The accuracy of phosphorescent imaging is closely related to the afterglow lifetime and luminous intensity of phosphorescent probes. Therefore, developing long-lived phosphorescent probes is key to phosphorescent imaging. Current RTP probes include organic transition metal complexes, organic molecules, and carbon dots (CDs) [12–14]. Among them, CDs stand out because they not only have the similar advantages of good phosphorescence with organic transition metal complexes, but also show better biocompatibility [15–18]. Li et al. obtained an RTP probe with a liquid phosphorescent lifetime of 1.46 s by coating phosphorescent CDs with water-soluble SiO2 [19]. The probe can be used for phosphorescent imaging of bean sprouts. Similarly, Liang et al. coated phosphorescent CDs with SiO2 to obtain RTP CDs complexes with good liquid-phase phosphorescent properties and a phosphorescent lifetime of 1.86 s, which can be applied to the phosphorescent imaging in vitro [20]. It can be found that RTP CD probes are usually modified by encapsulating a SiO2 layer on solid-state phosphorescent CDs to limit the vibration and rotation of their emission center, suppress non-radiative transitions, and isolate water and oxygen from contacting with hydrogen bond network and triplet excitons, thereby maintaining phosphorescence in aqueous solution [21–23].

However, not all solid-state RTP CDs possess phosphorescence in solution when they are encapsulated by SiO2. The key premise of achieving long-lived RTP CDs in solution is to synthesize long-lived solid-state RTP CDs. At present, the reported solid-state phosphorescent CDs can be divided into two categories according to their structures: CDs embedded in matrices [24] and CDs without matrices [25]. In the former case, CDs are embedded in various matrices, such as urea/biuret [26], boron trioxide [27], polyurethane [28], and polyvinyl alcohol [29]. Phosphorescence can be emitted owing to the restriction of rigid structure and the inhibition of non-radiative transitions caused by matrices. But the matrices for embedding CDs are always destroyed when they are dissolved in solution. In the latter case, CDs are self-protected by cross-linking enhanced emission effects (CEE) [30] or hydrogen bonding networks [31]. Their phosphorescence emission can be easily achieved by inhibition of non-radiative transitions. As the cross-linked structure or hydrogen bonding network on the surface of CDs without matrices could be protected by SiO2 layer, RTP CDs in solution are all prepared from solid-state phosphorescent CDs without matrices [20].

The synthesis of solid-state phosphorescent CDs without matrices based on polymers or small molecules is more likely to form tightly cross-linked structures or hydrogen-bonded networks on the surface of CDs during the carbonization and polymerization, thus providing the structural rigidity required for phosphorescent emission [32,33]. It was reported that the phosphorescent lifetime of matrix-free CDs synthesized from ethylenediamine + polyacrylic acid or ethylenediamine + phosphoric acid is about 1.3 s [19,20], which still needs to be improved to increase the imaging accuracy. Generally, two methods have been to obtain long-lived solid-state RTP CDs without matrices: (1) The introduction of heteroatoms (N, P, B, halogens, etc.) or carbonyl groups onto CDs, which can enhances spin-orbit coupling (SOC), thereby facilitating the intersystem crossing (ISC) process and enhancing phosphorescence emission [34–37]; (2) Restriction of molecular vibrations and rotations of CDs’ emission center, which can stabilizes triplet excitons, resulting in long-lived phosphorescent emission [38,39].

Herein, to obtain the phosphorescent CDs in solution with long lifetime for efficient bioimaging, B, N, P co-doped phosphorescent CDs without matrices (B, N, P-CDs) were in situ prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method from heteroatom-rich small molecules (boric acid, phosphoric acid, and ethylenediamine) as raw materials. The effect of the doping content of B, N, and P atoms on the structure and properties of long-lived phosphorescent CDs was investigated. Subsequently, liquid RTP was achieved by coating the B, N, P-CDs with SiO2 to obtain complex of CDs and silica (B, N, P-CDs@SiO2). Reasons for liquid RTP emission and long-lived liquid phosphorescence of CDs were elucidated. The obtained B, N, P-CDs and B, N, P-CDs@SiO2 in aqueous solution have a phosphorescent lifetime of 1.89 and 1.97 s, respectively, which is well ahead of the reported phosphorescence probes [19, 20, 22, 23, 40–42]. They also perform well in phosphorescent bioimaging, offering a prospect for developing advanced phosphorescent probes. ( Scheme 1).

Ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) was purchased from Tianjin Xingfu Technology Development Co. Ltd. Boric acid (H3BO3) was purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co. Ltd. Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) was purchased from Shanghai Taicang Hu Test Reagent Co. Ltd. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, C8H20O4Si) was purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co. Ltd. Ammonia (NH3·H2O) was purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Human hepatoellular carcinomas cells (HepG2) and hepatic cell line (HL-7702) cells were purchased from Shanghai Saibakang Biological Co. Ltd. Phosphate buffered salt (PBS) solution, trypsin, CCK-8 kit and culture medium were purchased from Wuhan PhD Biotechnology Co. Ltd. The BALB/c nude mice were purchased from Beijing Vitalihua Laboratory Animal Technology Co. Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEOL JEM 2100 electron microscope to characterize the particle size, morphology, and microstructure of CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns was measured on a DX-2700 X-ray diffractometer (copper target, 1.5418 Å) to obtain the crystal line structures of CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) absorption spectroscopy were recorded on a TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrometer to determine the molecular structure and the functional groups on the surface of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250 xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, which was also used to analyze the elemental components and functional groups on CDs. The optical properties were characterized by photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet absorption (UV-Vis) on a Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrometer and a 756 MC UV-Vis spectrophotometer, respectively.

Photoluminescence spectra were acquired on an FS5 spectrometer using a 150 W xenon lamp and a microsecond pulsed flash lamp as the excitation source. Absolute quantum yields were obtained on integrating spheres at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Phosphorescence decay curves were recorded on a Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrometer. Live cell imaging pictures were captured by TCS Model SP8 confocal microscopy. Live mouse imaging is photographed on a PerkinElmer type small animal live optical 3D imager.

First, phosphoric acid (0.4 mL, 0.0008 mol), boric acid (1.0 g, 0.0016 mol), and ethylenediamine (0.5 mL, 0.0008 mol) were accurately weighed and dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and then sonicated for 3 min to form a clear solution. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon reactor, sealed, and reacted at 280 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled naturally to room temperature. Subsequently, the reaction solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane, and the resulting filtrate was dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h in a dialysis bag with a MWCO of 500 Da to remove the impurities of small molecules. The dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain a pale-yellow powder, which is named as B, N, P-CDs280. B, N, P-CDs synthesized at 200 and 240 °C were named as B, N, P-CDs200 and B, N, P-CDs240, respectively.

Synthesis of B, N, P-CDs@SiO2 was referred to the previous work, and some improvements were made [43]. In brief, B, N, P-CDs280 (100 mg) were first dispersed in deionized water (50 mL), and followed by the addition of TEOS (1 mL) and ammonia (0.5 mL). After that, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL three-necked flask, stirred at 25 °C for 24 h. The reaction solution was first filtered through a membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm, and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3000 Da) with deionized water for 1 day to obtain the aqueous phase B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2. The solid B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 material was collected by drying the liquid in a vacuum oven heated to 80 °C for 12 h. B, N, P-CDs200 @SiO2 and B, N, P-CDs240@SiO2 were also synthesized in the same conditions as for B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2.

First, HepG2 cells were individually digested and dispersed in 10% fetal bovine serum medium (containing 0.1 % antigen, 1 % penicillin streptomycin mix (double antibody)). Second, they were added into imaging dishes (1 mL/well) and incubated for 24 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2). Third, the cells were washed with PBS solution for three times. After that, 100 μL of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 (100 μg/mL) was added and incubated for 2 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2). Finally, both fluorescent imaging and phosphorescent imaging photographs were taken with a laser confocal microscope (λex = 365 nm; λem, fluorescence = 385 −485 nm; λem, phosphorescence = 485 −585 nm).

BALB/c female nude mice were used for in vivo subcutaneous imaging. Mice were anesthetized with isoflurane, and then B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 solution (100 μL, 100 μg/mL) was injected into the dorsal region of the mice. B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 complex was activated with a handheld UV lamp for 1 min before in vivo imaging. Once the UV lamp was turned off, the mice were transferred to the IVIS in vivo imaging system, and signals were acquired in bioluminescence mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of B, N, P-CDs280
ce:section.ce:para: B, N, P-CDs280 in powder form exhibit blue emission under UV irradiation (365 nm) and green afterglow emission when the UV lamp is switched off ( Fig. 1a). The afterglow time is up to 18 s. TEM image and corresponding particle size distribution diagram show that B, N, P-CDs280 have a uniform particle size distribution and an average particle size of 3.07 nm. HRTEM image shows a lattice spacing of 0.212 nm, corresponding to graphitic carbon (100) crystal line plane (Fig. 1b). The XRD patterns of B, N, P-CDs280 show a typical diffraction peak centered at 24° (Fig. 1c), which is attributed to the (100) crystalline plane of graphitic carbon, in good agreement with HRTEM results [44]. The diffraction peak at 12° is attributed to the polymeric cross-linked backbone of the CDs, consistent with the characteristics of carbonized polymer dots [45]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the FTIR spectra of B, N, P-CDs280 show broad absorption at 3750 − 2650 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibrations of −OH, −NH2, and methylene −CH2 − groups [46]. Three absorption bands are observed at 1680, 1470 and 1400 cm−1, associated with the stretching vibrations of CC, N − H, and C−N bonds, respectively [47]. The characteristic bands located at 1184, 999, and 812 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of P = O, N − P, and B−O bonds, respectively [48].

The compositions on the surface of B, N, P-CDs were investigated by XPS, and the elemental and bonding information were analyzed in detail (Fig. 1e). The full XPS spectra show five typical peaks representing C 1 s (284.8 eV), N 1s (399.4 eV), O 1s (531.6 eV), B 1s (192.2 eV), and P 2p (133.1 eV), indicating that B, N, P-CDs280 are composed mainly of the elements B, C, N, O and P [49,50]. All FTIR and XPS results (as shown in Supporting information 2.1) suggest that B, N, P-CDs280 are rich in heteroatomic functional groups on their surface.

Optical properties of B, N, P-CDs280 were analyzed on a UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrometer. UV absorption spectra show two absorption peaks at 280 and 350 nm, which are probably caused by π − π * transition of C−C/CC and n − π * transition of CN/CO, respectively (Fig. 1f) [51]. The emission of fluorescence and the emission of phosphorescence at the same excitation wavelength also demonstrate that B, N, P-CDs280 emit phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence. The fluorescence emission of B, N, P-CDs280 is excitation-dependent in blue region (Fig. 1g), while phosphorescence emission of B, N, P-CDs280 is excitation-independent with optimal excitation at 345 nm (Fig. 1h). The reasons for their fluorescent excitation dependence and phosphorescent excitation independence are explained by the variety of surface luminescent functional groups of CDs (Supporting information 2.1, Fig. S6). According to the lifetime decay curve (Fig. 1i), the lifetime τavg could be calculated by the following equation [52]:
(2)
τ
avg
=
∑
τ
i
2
α
i
∕
∑
τ
i
α
i
where, τi is the decay time and α
i is the pre-exponential factor of corresponding τi. B, N, P-CDs280 have a long phosphorescent lifetime of 1.89 s, which is well above that of most current solid-state phosphorescent CDs (Table S1) [53–55]. These results indicate that B, N, P-CDs280 have excellent solid-state phosphorescent emission properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of heteroatom covalent structure on the long-lived phosphorescent properties of B, N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The B, N, P-CDs280 obtained from above experiments have shown excellent phosphorescent properties. To get further insight into the effects of heteroatom covalent structure on the long-lived phosphorescent properties of B, N, P-CDs, two sets of experiments were designed: 1) Effects of raw material on phosphorescent emission, where the role of dopants is discussed, as show in Supporting information 2.2; 2) Effects of reaction temperature on phosphorescent emission, where reaction temperature is regulated (200 and 240 °C) to synthesize B, N, P-CDs200 and B, N, P-CDs240 with different covalent structures [56]. According to the results shown in Supporting information 2.3, B, N, P-CDs were obtained with ethylenediamine as carbon source, boric acid and phosphoric acid as cross-linking agents for polymerization reaction. The twisted polymer chains attach on surface of CDs (as shown in Fig. 2a).

As the hydrothermal reaction becomes more intense with increasing reaction temperature, more boric acid and phosphoric acid participate in the reaction, further improving the electronic properties of the CDs and forming a more closely cross-linked structure on carbon nuclei (Fig. 2a). Therefore, according to the effects of raw materials and reaction temperature on phosphorescent emission, a plausible the phosphorescent emission mechanism of solid-state phosphorescent CDs of B, N, P-CDs280 can be drafted as follows:

(1) The luminescence of CDs is derived from the co-doping of B, N, and P atoms, especially, the introduction of B plays a key role in promoting the transition of singlet excitons to triplet states (as demonstrated in Supporting information 2.3). As B atom is electrophilic, the introduction of B into CDs can reduce the energy gap (ΔE ST) between the singlet and triplet states, which is beneficial to the conversion of singlet excitons to triplet excitons through spin inversion. Moreover, with the increase of reaction temperatures, the number of heteroatoms (B and P atoms) combined in CDs gradually increases, which, to some extent, enhances the spin-orbit coupling of CDs’ emission center, further reducing the energy level difference between single and triplet states [57] and finally promoting the ISC process. ΔE ST can be calculated as follows:
ΔE ST=E S–E T=1240/λem, FL– 1240/λem, Phos·

where λem, FL and λem, Phos. are fluorescence and phosphorescent emission optimal of CDs at 77 K, respectively. The analysis of the 77 K low-temperature fluorescence phosphorescence spectra (Fig. S14) shows that the ΔE ST of the CDs decreases from 0.65, 0.59–0.47 eV by increasing the reaction temperature from 200, 240–280 °C (Fig. 2b), which also indicates a significant reduction in the energy level difference between the single and triplet states [58]. Smaller ΔE ST value is more conducive to achieving effective ISC process and satisfying the filling of excitons, resulting in enhanced phosphorescence performance (Fig. 2b) [59].

(2) As the temperature increases, the number of heteroatoms and their functional groups increase, including N − H, B−O, and P − O/P = O, which induces more compact cross-linked structure on the surface of CDs to form a tighter hydrogen bonding network (Fig. 2a) [60]. It can improve the structural rigidity of CDs, limit the molecular vibrations and suppress non-radiative leaps, thus improving the phosphorescent properties of CDs. To demonstrate the existence of a hydrogen bonding network in CDs, they were dissolved in different organic solvents and water. Phosphorescence was observed in organic solvents, but not observed in water, which illustrates that water molecules may break the hydrogen bonding network among CDs. Therefore, the vibration and rotation of CDs’ emission centers are unrestricted and the non-radiative transition is enhanced to quench the phosphorescence [48,60]. In contrast, organic solvents can not break the hydrogen bonding network of CDs, thus allowing CDs exhibit phosphorescence in organic solvents (Support Information 2.4, Video S1 and S2).

In general, the reduced ΔE ST makes it easier for singlet exciton to transfer to triplet exciton, and the compact cross-linked structure and firm hydrogen bonding network can restrict the vibration and rotation of CDs’ emission centers to suppress non-radiative transition. These combined effects promote the increased phosphorescent quantum yield (QYP) and lifetime of CDs [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para:
Although B, N, P-CDs280 have good phosphorescent properties in solid state, their solution only emits fluorescence under UV irradiation and does not emit phosphorescence when the excitation source is removed ( Fig. 3a). In order to achieve long-lived emission in liquid phase, this work uses the Stöber method to add a protective silica layer on the surface of the solid-state phosphorescent CDs. The silica layer has an important role of isolating water and oxygen [62]. The afterglow lifetime of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 is 12 s, which is much longer than that of B, N, P-CDs200 @SiO2 and B, N, P-CDs240 @SiO2 (Supporting information 2.5, Fig. S15).
(λex = 345 nm and λem = 502 nm)

The morphology and structure of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 were characterized. As shown in the TEM image, B, N, P-CDs280 are well dispersed inside SiO2 matrix (Fig. 3b). The FTIR spectra of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 show the presence of Si−O−H (3454 cm−1) and Si−O−Si (1089 cm−1) stretching vibration bands, indicating that B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 has the characteristic bands of SiO2. In addition, the characteristic vibrational bands centered at 890 and 1456 cm−1 are attributed to N − Si and C−Si bonds, respectively (Fig. 3c), which suggests that covalent bonds are formed between B, N, P-CDs280 and SiO2 by covalent bonding [63]. The corresponding XPS spectra further illustrate the presence of Si in B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 (Fig. 3d). High resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, P 2p, and Si 2p were also measured, as shown in the Supporting information. The high-resolution Si 2p spectra show three peaks at 103.1, 103.7, and 104.3 eV, corresponding to Si−O, Si−C and Si−Ox bonds, respectively (Fig. 3e) [19]. These results show that B, N, P-CDs280 are covalently bonded to SiO2 and confined inside SiO2 matrix.

The optical properties of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 solution were also characterized by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence analysis. As shown in Fig. 3f, for the UV–vis spectra of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2, the two absorption peaks at 280 and 350 nm may be caused by the π − π * transition of C−C/CC and the n − π * transition of CN/CO, respectively, in agreement with the behavior of B, N, P-CDs280. From the fluorescence and phosphorescence excitation spectra of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2, it is known that the fluorescent and phosphorescent emissions of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 are located at the same emission centers. The fluorescence of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 is consistent with that of B, N, P-CDs280 in solid powder, with the optimal fluorescence emission of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 located at 390 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 345 nm (Fig. 3g). As shown in Fig. 3h, the RTP of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 exhibits excitation independence, and has the optimal emission at 502 nm when the optimal excitation wavelength is 345 nm. The fluorescent QY and QYP of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 are 40.60 % and 3.15 %, respectively. The QYP is better than the average of most phosphorescent probes (as shown in Table S4), demonstrating the advantages of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 for bioimaging. The phosphorescence lifetime decay curve for B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 taken at 502 nm is shown in Fig. 3i. B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 have a phosphorescence lifetime of 1.97 s, which is the longest reported for phosphorescent CDs in aqueous solution to our knowledge ( Table 1) [19–23, 40–42, 64, 65].

The mechanism of the long-lived properties was explored. B, N, P-CDs280 are encapsulated in SiO2 matrix by covalent bonds C−Si and N − Si. In aqueous solution, hydrogen bonds are formed to further fasten the covalent network, so that the movement and rotation of the B, N, P-CDs280 emission centers are greatly restricted, thus contributing to the reduction of non-radiative transitions ( Fig. 4a) [23]. In addition, the SiO2 shell can separate B, N, P-CDs280 from the triplet oxygen in solution, preventing the triplet exciton energy from being deactivated by collisions with the triplet oxygen to transform themselves to singlet oxygen. The SiO2 covalent network can restrict the non-radiative transition of B, N, P-CDs280 and isolates the triplet oxygen, allowing a slower non-radiative transition rate and avoidance of triplet exciton inactivation [66]. Therefore, the phosphorescence lifetime of B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 in water is longer than that of solid B, N, P-CDs280 (Fig. 4b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging properties of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Before imaging, low cytotoxicity of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 was confirmed by CCK-8 (as shown in Supporting Information 2.6, Fig. S17). HepG2 cells were incubated with B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 to investigate their imaging properties, and the results is shown in Fig. 5. A bright field image of HepG2 cells are shown in Fig. 5a, and the fluorescent image of HepG2 cells excited at 365 nm in Fig. 5b. The blue emission signal of the cells is detected between 385 − 485 nm, indicating that B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 are distributed in the cytoplasm of HepG2 cells. The phosphorescent image of HepG2 cells excited at 365 nm is shown in Fig. 5c, with a detection range between 485 − 585 nm. A bright green light is observed. In Fig. 5d for the overlapping images of bright field, fluorescence and phosphorescence, B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 are distributed into cytoplasm of HepG2 cells uniformly [40]. This illustrates that B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 can be used for phosphorescence imaging in vitro in cells by their long-lived afterglow.

Moreover, B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 were used as phosphorescent imaging agents for afterglow imaging in vivo. The images were recorded in bioluminescence mode by an IVIS in vivo imager. First, phosphorescent signals were collected after removal of the excitation source by irradiating different concentrations of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 aqueous solution with UV light for 1 min. The phosphorescence emission of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 was relatively easy to collect as shown in Fig. 5e and the phosphorescence intensity increases gradually with the increase of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 concentration, showing good phosphorescence properties. Second, to confirm their potential for phosphorescent imaging in vivo, B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 in solution form were injected under the dorsal skin of mouse. For control, water was also injected into the dorsal skin of another mouse. The dorsal skin of the mice was then irradiated with a UV lamp (365 nm) for 1 min. After removing the UV excitation light source, images were taken, as shown in Fig. 5f. No signal was detected on the mouse injected with water (control) (the left of Fig. 5f), while obvious signals were obtained on the mouse injected with B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 solution. The average luminous intensity of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 in region of interest (ROI) is 7.70 × 105 p/s/cm2/sr, which is much higher than the value (1.61 × 104 p/s/cm2/sr) reported in current literature concerning RTP CDs [20]. Moreover, the maximum phosphorescent intensity of N, B, P-CDs@SiO2 reaches 2.50 × 106 p/s/cm2/sr, which is a very high value compared with most of organic and inorganic phosphorescent probes, as shown in Table S4 [67–73]. In addition, in the video of subcutaneous imaging in vivo, the afterglow time of N, B, P-CDs@SiO2 observed with the naked eye is 4 s (Video S3), showing superiority to other RTP CDs [20]. All these results show that B, N, P-CDs280 @SiO2 with long lifetime can be used as an excellent phosphorescent probe for afterglow bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo.

In summary, this work developed a new strategy for the synthesis of long-lived room-temperature phosphorescent complex of B, N, P co-doped CDs and silica. First, solid-state matrix-free phosphorescent CDs were prepared from boric acid, phosphoric acid, and ethylenediamine by hydrothermal treatment. The phosphorescent properties of B, N, P-CDs can be tuned by the raw materials with different heteroatoms and reaction temperature. The co-doping of heteroatoms (N, B, and P atoms) in CDs decreases ΔE ST between the singlet and triplet states, and enhance the cross-linked structure and hydrogen bonding network of CDs, thus improving their phosphorescent properties. Especially, B atom plays a key role in promoting the transition of singlet excitons to triplet states. When the hydrothermal temperature increases from 200° to 280 °C, the phosphorescent lifetime increases from 718.79 ms to 1.89 s correspondingly. B, N, P-CDs280 have much higher phosphorescent lifetime than most current solid-state phosphorescent CDs without matrices. Second, B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 were obtained by coating B, N, P-CDs280 with silica, in which the network constructed by Si−O bond can well limit the non-radiative transition of CDs and protect the luminescent groups on the surface of B, N, P-CDs280 by isolating water and triplet oxygen, thus improving the phosphorescence lifetime and phosphorescence of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2. The phosphor lifetime in aqueous solution was increased to 1.97 s, which is the longest for reported phosphorescent CDs in aqueous solution to our knowledge. Finally, the application of B, N, P-CDs280@SiO2 in bioimaging was demonstrated by taking advantage of outstanding optical properties and low toxicity. This work provides a prerequisite for further biomedical applications of phosphorescent CDs.

Lin Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis. Shaoban Zhao: Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing–Original Draft, Visualization. Yan Wang: Data curation. Shiping Yu: Validation, Funding acquisition. Yongzhen Yang: Writing – Review & Editing. Xuguang Liu: Review & Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Foundational Research Project of Shanxi Province (202203021211159), Shanxi-Zheda Institute of Advanced Materials and Chemical Engineering (2021SX-FR010), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022–039), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51972221 and 82172048), Shanxi Technology Innovation Center for Controlled and Sustained Release of Nano-drugs (202104010911026). Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory for Electronic Functional Materials and Devices (EFMD2021006M).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-protective room-temperature phosphorescence of fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Long, P.; Feng, Y.; Cao, C., et al., Self-protective room-temperature phosphorescence of fluorine and nitrogen codoped carbon dots. Advanced Functional Materials, 2018, 28 (37), 1800791.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots confined in 3D polymer network: producing robust room temperature phosphorescence with tunable lifetimes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhai, Y.; Wang, P.; Zhang, X., et al., Carbon dots confined in 3D polymer network: Producing robust room temperature phosphorescence with tunable lifetimes. Chinese Chemical Letters, 2022, 33 (2), 783−787.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controlled growth of monodisperse silica spheres in the micron size range

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Stöber, W.; Fink, A.; Bohn, E., Controlled growth of monodisperse silica spheres in the micron size range. Journal of Colloid and Interface Science, 1968, 26 (1), 62−69.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of a photoluminescent nanoparticle based on fullerene-silica hybridization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jeong, J.; Cho, M.; Lim, Y.T., et al., Synthesis and characterization of a photoluminescent nanoparticle based on fullerene-silica hybridization. Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 2009, 48 (29), 5296−5299.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategy for activating room-temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots in aqueous environments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao, Y.; Zhang, H.; Jiao, Y., et al., Strategy for activating room-temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots in aqueous environments. Chemistry of Materials, 2019, 31 (19), 7979−7986.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activating room temperature long afterglow of carbon dots via covalent fixation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiang, K.; Wang, Y.; Cai, C., et al., Activating room temperature long afterglow of carbon dots via covalent fixation. Chemistry of Materials, 2017, 29 (11), 4866−4873.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of covalent bond engineered encapsulation structure toward efficient, long-lived multicolored phosphorescent carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Y.; Li, M.; Lu, S., Rational design of covalent bond engineered encapsulation structure toward efficient, long-lived multicolored phosphorescent carbon dots. Small, 2022, 2206080.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong phosphorescence of water-soluble organic nanoparticles for in vivo afterglow imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhen, X.; Tao, Y.; An Z., et al., Ultralong phosphorescence of water-soluble organic nanoparticles for in vivo afterglow imaging. Advanced Materials, 2017, 29 (33): 1606665.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The odd–even effect of alkyl chain in organic room temperature phosphorescence luminogens and the corresponding in vivo imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, J.; Gao, H.; Wang, Y., et al., The odd–even effect of alkyl chain in organic room temperature phosphorescence luminogens and the corresponding in vivo imaging. Materials Chemistry Frontiers, 2019, 3 (7): 1391−1397.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A twin-axial pseudorotaxane for phosphorescence cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ma, X.K.; Zhang, Y.M.; Yu, Q., et al., A twin-axial pseudorotaxane for phosphorescence cell imaging. Chemical Communications, 2021, 57(10): 1214−1217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phosphorescent starburst Pt (II) porphyrins as bifunctional therapeutic agents for tumor hypoxia imaging and photodynamic therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lv, Z.; Zou, L.; Wei, H., et al., Phosphorescent starburst Pt (II) porphyrins as bifunctional therapeutic agents for tumor hypoxia imaging and photodynamic therapy. ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 2018, 10 (23): 19523−19533.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhodium (I) complex-based polymeric nanomicelles in water exhibiting coexistent near-infrared phosphorescence imaging and anticancer activity in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, J.; Nie, J.J.; Guo, P., et al., Rhodium (I) complex-based polymeric nanomicelles in water exhibiting coexistent near-infrared phosphorescence imaging and anticancer activity in vivo. Journal of the American Chemical Society, 2020, 142(6): 2709−2714.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Guest-host doped strategy for constructing ultralong-lifetime near-infrared organic phosphorescence materials for bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xiao, F.; Gao, H.; Lei, Y., et al., Guest-host doped strategy for constructing ultralong-lifetime near-infrared organic phosphorescence materials for bioimaging. Nature Communications, 2022, 13(1): 186.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Millisecond-range time-resolved bioimaging enabled through ultralong aqueous phosphorescence probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cui, M.; Dai, P.; Ding, J., et al., Millisecond-range time-resolved bioimaging enabled through ultralong aqueous phosphorescence probes. Angewandte Chemie, 2022, 134(14): e202200172.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lin Chen received her Ph.D. degree from Taiyuan University of Technology in 2015. She is now an assistant professor in the Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, China. Her main researches focus on optical carbon functional materials.

Shaoban Zhao received his bachelor's degree from Taiyuan University of Technology in 2020. He is now a master at Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, China. His research interest focuses on phosphorescent probe for bioimaging.

Yan Wang received his bachelor's degree from Hubei College of Medicine in 2020. He is now a master at Intervention Department of the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China. His main researches focus on the bioimaging and cancer therapy.

Shiping Yu received his Ph.D. degree from Chinese Medical Sciences University in 2005. He is currently a chief physician in Intervention Department of the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China. His research interest includes the synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in cancer therapy.

Yongzhen Yang received her Ph. D. degree from Taiyuan University of Technology in 2007. She is now a professor in the Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, Ministry of Education, Taiyuan University of Technology, China. Her researches focus on functional carbon nanomaterials.

Xuguang Liu received his Ph. D. degree from Taiyuan University of Technology in 2000. He is now an assistant professor in the College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, China. His research interests include carbon nanomaterials, separation nanomaterials and energy materials.
